content: Animal classification groups living organisms by shared traits and evolutionary relationships. Vertebrates have backbones and include mammals, birds, reptiles, amphibians, and fish. Invertebrates do not have backbones and include insects, spiders, mollusks, and many marine animals. Good questions should avoid treating all invertebrates as insects or all aquatic animals as fish.

Adaptations are inherited traits or behaviors that help organisms survive and reproduce in particular environments. Camouflage helps some animals avoid predators, migration helps animals track food or breeding conditions, and hibernation lowers energy use during scarce seasons. Strong questions should distinguish adaptation from a temporary habit learned by one individual.

Music notation represents pitch, rhythm, duration, and expression on a staff. Notes placed higher on the staff usually indicate higher pitch, while note shapes and rests describe timing. A time signature such as 4/4 organizes beats into measures. Good educational questions should distinguish tempo, which is speed, from pitch, which is how high or low a sound is.

Musical instruments are often grouped by how they produce sound. String instruments vibrate strings, woodwinds use vibrating air columns or reeds, brass instruments use lip vibration into a mouthpiece, percussion instruments are struck or shaken, and electronic instruments generate or modify signals. Strong questions should avoid grouping instruments only by appearance.

Democracy is a system of government in which political power is derived from the people, either directly or through elected representatives. Free and fair elections, civil liberties, rule of law, and accountable institutions are common democratic principles. Good questions should distinguish democracy from monarchy, dictatorship, and one-time voting without rights protections.

Separation of powers divides government authority among branches such as legislative, executive, and judicial institutions. The legislative branch makes laws, the executive branch carries them out, and the judicial branch interprets laws and resolves disputes. Checks and balances are mechanisms that prevent one branch from becoming too powerful.

Film production usually moves through development, pre-production, production, post-production, and distribution. Development shapes the concept and script, pre-production plans cast and locations, production records footage, post-production edits picture and sound, and distribution brings the film to audiences. Strong questions should not confuse filming with the whole production process.

Cinematography is the craft of capturing moving images through camera placement, lens choice, lighting, framing, movement, and exposure. A close-up can emphasize emotion or detail, while a wide shot can establish setting and spatial relationships. Good questions should separate cinematography from acting, editing, and screenwriting.

Measurement uses standard units to describe quantities such as length, mass, time, temperature, and volume. The metric system is based on powers of ten, with units such as meter, gram, and liter. Accuracy describes closeness to the true value, while precision describes consistency between repeated measurements.

International institutions coordinate cooperation between countries on issues such as peace, health, trade, culture, and finance. The United Nations was founded in 1945 after World War II and includes a General Assembly and Security Council. Good questions should avoid implying that one institution controls every country or replaces national governments.

Team sports use shared rules to coordinate players, scoring, timing, boundaries, and fouls. Soccer primarily uses feet and rewards goals, basketball advances a ball by dribbling and passing, and volleyball sends a ball over a net without holding it. Good questions should identify the sport-specific rule instead of asking vague facts about competition.

The modern Olympic Games are an international multi-sport event inspired by ancient Greek athletic festivals. The Summer and Winter Olympics are held on separate cycles and include athletes representing National Olympic Committees. Strong questions should distinguish the Olympics from single-sport world championships.

Color theory studies how colors relate, contrast, and combine in visual design. Primary colors differ by color model: red, yellow, and blue are common in traditional art teaching, while red, green, and blue are used for additive light on screens. Complementary colors sit opposite each other on many color wheels and can create strong contrast.

Art conservation preserves artworks by controlling light, humidity, temperature, handling, and restoration choices. Museums document provenance, materials, condition, and exhibition history. Conservation aims to slow damage and keep evidence intact, not to make an artwork look brand new at any cost.

Cells are the basic units of life. Eukaryotic cells have a nucleus and membrane-bound organelles, while prokaryotic cells such as bacteria lack a nucleus. DNA stores genetic instructions, ribosomes build proteins, and mitochondria release usable energy in many eukaryotic cells. Good questions should avoid saying every cell has every organelle.

Electricity involves the movement or presence of electric charge. Current measures the flow of charge, voltage measures electric potential difference, and resistance opposes current. In a simple circuit, a power source, conductive path, and load such as a lamp are needed for continuous current flow.

Ancient Egypt developed along the Nile River, whose seasonal flooding supported agriculture. Egyptian society built monumental architecture, used hieroglyphic writing, practiced complex religious traditions, and was ruled by pharaohs. Good questions should avoid reducing Egyptian history only to pyramids or treating all periods as identical.

The Roman Republic existed before the Roman Empire and included institutions such as consuls, the Senate, assemblies, and written laws. Roman citizenship, military expansion, social conflict, and political rivalry shaped its development. Julius Caesar's rise and assassination were part of the crisis that ended republican rule.

Map skills include reading scale, symbols, legends, coordinates, direction, and projection. Latitude measures north-south position relative to the equator, while longitude measures east-west position relative to the prime meridian. Good questions should not confuse a map legend with a story legend.

Rivers shape landscapes by erosion, transport, and deposition of sediment. A river basin or watershed drains water toward a common outlet, while deltas can form where rivers slow and deposit sediment near a larger body of water. Strong questions should distinguish river source, mouth, tributary, and watershed.

Fractions represent parts of a whole or ratios between quantities. Equivalent fractions have the same value even if numerator and denominator differ, such as 1/2 and 2/4. Adding fractions with different denominators usually requires a common denominator. Good questions should separate numerator, denominator, and value.

Statistics uses data to describe patterns and uncertainty. The mean is the arithmetic average, the median is the middle value when data are ordered, and the mode is the most frequent value. A sample is part of a population; biased samples can lead to misleading conclusions.

Computer networks connect devices so they can exchange data. The internet is a global network of networks that uses protocols such as IP for addressing and routing. Wi-Fi is a wireless local networking technology, while the web is a service that uses the internet to deliver pages and applications.

Cybersecurity protects systems, networks, and data from unauthorized access, disruption, or misuse. Strong passwords, multi-factor authentication, software updates, backups, and phishing awareness reduce common risks. Good questions should avoid providing attack instructions and should focus on safe defensive principles.

Figurative language uses words beyond their literal meaning to create effect. A simile compares using words such as like or as, a metaphor describes one thing as another, and personification gives human traits to non-human things. Strong questions should use short examples and ask learners to identify the device.

Non-fiction arguments use claims, reasons, evidence, and counterarguments to persuade or explain. A claim states a position, evidence supports it, and reasoning connects evidence to the claim. Good educational questions should distinguish evidence from opinion and avoid implying that every persuasive text is fiction.

Video game genres describe common play patterns rather than hardware. Puzzle games emphasize problem solving, role-playing games emphasize character progression and choices, platformers emphasize movement through spaces, and strategy games emphasize planning and resource decisions. Many games combine multiple genres.

Game accessibility features help more players interact with games. Examples include remappable controls, subtitles, colorblind-friendly palettes, difficulty options, text scaling, and audio cues. Accessibility improves usability without necessarily making a game less challenging for every player.
